hints: Attach an indicator to each of the 5 toy types: 1 if you collect it.
E[distinct] = 5·P(a given toy appears in ≥1 of 7 boxes) = 5·(1−(4/5)⁷).
solution:
Let $I_i=1$ if toy $i$ is collected. By symmetry each toy is missed in all $7$ boxes with probability $(4/5)^7$, so $\mathbb{E}[I_i]=1-(4/5)^7$. By linearity,
$$\mathbb{E}[\text{distinct}]=5\Big(1-\big(\tfrac45\big)^7\Big)=5\Big(1-\tfrac{16384}{78125}\Big)=\tfrac{61741}{15625}.$$

hints: Both slopes involve the same correlation r: β_x=r·σ_y/σ_x and β_y=r·σ_x/σ_y.
From β_x=1 and σ_y/σ_x=√(20/10)=√2, get r=1/√2.
solution: For simple regression, $\beta_x=r\dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_x}$ and $\beta_y=r\dfrac{\sigma_x}{\sigma_y}$. With $\sigma_x=\sqrt{10},\sigma_y=\sqrt{20}$, $\dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_x}=\sqrt2$, so $\beta_x=r\sqrt2=1\Rightarrow r=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt2}$. Then $\beta_y=r\dfrac{\sigma_x}{\sigma_y}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt2}\cdot\dfrac{1}{\sqrt2}=\tfrac12.$ (Also note $\beta_x\beta_y=r^2$.)

hints: Recall Black–Scholes gamma for an ATM option and how it scales with the underlying price.
Gamma = φ(d₁)/(Sσ√T); ATM means S=K, so gamma ∝ 1/(K·σ√T).
Lowest strike has highest gamma ⇒ K=10.
solution: Black–Scholes gamma is $\Gamma=\dfrac{\varphi(d_1)}{S\sigma\sqrt T}$. For ATM options $S=K$ and $d_1$ is essentially the same across them, so $\Gamma\propto \dfrac{1}{K}$. The smallest strike therefore has the largest gamma: $K=10.$

hints: Apply Itô to W_t^{10}; the dt term must be cancelled by a_t.
d(W^{10}) = 10W⁹dW + ½·10·9·W⁸ dt = 10W⁹dW + 45W⁸ dt.
solution: By Itô, $d(W_t^{10})=10W_t^{9}\,dW_t+\tfrac12\cdot10\cdot9\,W_t^{8}\,dt=10W_t^{9}dW_t+45W_t^{8}dt.$ Then $dX_t=10W_t^{9}dW_t+(45W_t^{8}-a_t)dt$ is driftless (a martingale) iff $a_t=45W_t^{8}$. So $A=45,\,B=8$ and $AB=360.$

hints: A product is odd only when both factors are odd; with three odds total, you can have at most one odd product.
The sum is odd ⟺ exactly one of the three pairs is odd·odd.
Count: 3 pair-slots × (6 ways to place 2 odds) × (4! for the rest)=432 of 720 ⇒ 3/5.
solution:
A pair's product is odd iff both numbers are odd. There are three odd values $\{1,3,5\}$, so at most one pair can be odd·odd, and the sum of the three products is odd iff exactly one pair is odd·odd.

Choose which of the 3 pairs is the odd·odd one (3 ways), fill it with 2 of the 3 odds in order ($3\cdot2=6$ ways); the remaining $1$ odd $+3$ evens fill the other four slots in $4!=24$ ways (no further odd·odd possible). Favorable $=3\cdot6\cdot24=432$ out of $6!=720$, giving $\tfrac{432}{720}=\tfrac35.$

hints: Let t,h,b be tail, head, body; write each relation as an equation.
t=9, h=t+b/2, b=h+t. Substitute to solve for b.
solution: Let $t,h,b$ be the tail, head, body weights. Given $t=9$, $h=t+\tfrac{b}{2}$, and $b=h+t$. Substituting $h$: $b=(9+\tfrac{b}{2})+9=18+\tfrac{b}{2}\Rightarrow \tfrac{b}{2}=18\Rightarrow b=36$. Then $h=9+18=27$, and the fish weighs $t+h+b=9+27+36=72$ ounces.

hints: Track one card: position x maps to 2x mod 53 each shuffle.
The deck returns when 2^k ≡ 1 (mod 53), i.e. k = multiplicative order of 2 mod 53.
Since 2^26 ≡ −1 (mod 53), the order is 52 ⇒ 52 shuffles.
solution: Label positions $1$–$52$. This out-shuffle sends position $x$ to $2x \bmod 53$ (and $0$ never occurs since $53$ is prime). After $k$ shuffles a card is at $2^k x \bmod 53$, so the deck is restored when $2^k\equiv1\pmod{53}$ — the multiplicative order of $2$ mod $53$. Now $2^{13}\equiv30$ and $2^{26}\equiv30^2\equiv-1\pmod{53}$, so the order is $52$. Hence $52$ shuffles are needed.

hints: Pad numbers to 6 digits: consider 000000–999999, then add 1,000,000 separately.
Across 000000–999999 each of 6 positions shows each digit 0–9 exactly 100000 times.
solution: Write every number from $000000$ to $999999$ with six digits. In each of the $6$ positions, each digit $0$–$9$ appears exactly $100000$ times, contributing $100000\cdot(0+1+\dots+9)=100000\cdot45$. Over all positions that is $6\cdot100000\cdot45=27{,}000{,}000$ (this includes $000000$, which adds $0$, i.e. it covers $1$–$999999$). Finally $1{,}000{,}000$ adds digit sum $1$, giving $27{,}000{,}001.$

hints: X = position of the 5th king among 520 cards (40 kings) — negative hypergeometric. Maximize P(X=k), not E[X].
P(X=k) ∝ C(k−1,4)·C(520−k,35); examine the ratio P(k+1)/P(k).
Ratio ≥1 ⟺ k≤53.3, and P(54)>P(53)>… so the mode is k=54.
solution:
$X$, the position of the $5$th king, satisfies $P(X=k)\propto\binom{k-1}{4}\binom{520-k}{35}$ (4 kings in the first $k-1$ cards, a king at $k$, 35 kings after). Compare consecutive terms:
$$\frac{P(X=k+1)}{P(X=k)}=\frac{k}{k-4}\cdot\frac{485-k}{520-k}.$$
This exceeds $1$ iff $k(485-k)\ge(k-4)(520-k)$, i.e. $2080\ge39k$, i.e. $k\le53.3$. So the probabilities increase through $k=54$ and decrease after, making the mode $k=54.$

hints: Translate to congruences: n≡1 (mod 3), n≡3 (mod 5), n≡3 (mod 4), with n≤52.
Only 43 satisfies n≡1 (mod 3).
solution: We need $n\equiv1\ (\mathrm{mod}\ 3)$, $n\equiv3\ (\mathrm{mod}\ 5)$, $n\equiv3\ (\mathrm{mod}\ 4)$, with $n\le52$. The last two give $n\equiv3\ (\mathrm{mod}\ 20)$, so $n\in\{3,23,43\}$. Of these only $43$ satisfies $n\equiv1\ (\mathrm{mod}\ 3)$. So the deck has $43$ cards.

hints: Rewrite W₁+W₂ using independent increments.
W₁+W₂ = 2W₁ + (W₂−W₁), and W₁ ⟂ (W₂−W₁).
Var = 4·Var(W₁) + Var(W₂−W₁) = 4·1 + 1 = 5.
solution: Write $W_1+W_2=2W_1+(W_2-W_1)$. The increment $W_2-W_1$ is independent of $W_1$, with $\mathrm{Var}(W_1)=1$ and $\mathrm{Var}(W_2-W_1)=1$. Hence $\mathrm{Var}(W_1+W_2)=4\cdot1+1=5.$

hints: Note n+3 dominates n (beats everything n beats, plus n+2), so play is pushed to the top few values.
On {98,99,100} the win rule is a cycle: 100 beats 99, 99 beats 98, but 98 beats 100 (since 98 = 100−2).
Optimal is uniform 1/3 on {98,99,100}; Var of uniform on three consecutive integers = 2/3.
solution: Selecting $n+3$ weakly dominates $n$: it beats everything $n$ beats and also beats $n+2$ (via the 'two below' rule). Iterating pushes all weight to the top, leaving $\{98,99,100\}$. Among these the outcomes cycle: $100$ beats $99$ (higher), $99$ beats $98$ (higher), but $98$ beats $100$ since $98=100-2$. This is rock–paper–scissors, so the optimal mixed strategy is uniform $\tfrac13$ on each. The variance of a uniform distribution on three consecutive integers $\{98,99,100\}$ equals that of $\{-1,0,1\}$: $\tfrac{(-1)^2+0+1^2}{3}=\tfrac23.$
